data.title: U.S. Q4 Growth Set To Extend Streak As K-Shaped Risk Lurks
data.description:
The US economy is on track to report a third straight quarter of growth in tomorrow’s delayed GDP update for Q4, based on the median of a set of nowcasts compiled by The Capital Spectator. The pace is expected to slow from Q3, but the increase will be strong enough to keep last year’s chatter about recession on the fringes of economic analysis.

Today’s revised estimate shows output in the previous quarter rose 2.7% at an annualized rate for GDP, unchanged from the previous estimate. If this median nowcast is accurate, growth will downshift from Q3’s strong 4.4% advance, which marked a two-year high.

The widely followed GDPNow estimate from the Atlanta Fed remains the upside outlier, currently nowcasting a 3.6% increase. Using this outlook as a guide in context with our median nowcast implies that a high-2%-to-low-3% increase is a reasonable assumption for tomorrow’s release.

Although the top-line measure of economic activity is expected to chug along at a solid pace for a third straight quarter, there are growing concerns about the so-called K-economy effect - a reference to an uneven economy across sectors and households. For example, a substantial gap in consumer sentiment has opened up between consumers with equity investments compared with households with little or no stock holdings. “Sentiment surged [in February] for consumers with the largest stock portfolios, while it stagnated and remained at dismal levels for consumers without stock holdings,” survey director Joanne Hsu said.

The gap between high and low earners “leaves the economy much more sensitive,” said Samuel Tombs, chief US economist at Pantheon Macroeconomics. “It’s almost like the stock market is the tail that’s wagging the dog of the economy,” added Emily Roland, co-chief investment strategist at Manulife John Hancock Investments.

The implication: a sharp fall in the stock market could have outsized effects on the economy. That’s a risk for 2026, but the threat is expected to remain muted in tomorrow’s GDP update.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: GBP/USD Breaks Trendline; Is A 470-Odd Pip Decline On The Way? (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

GBP:USD has continued to slide, thanks in part to the US dollar resurgence this week as well as renewed hopes of rate cuts from the Bank of England (BoE).

For more on the fundamentals affecting the British pound and outlook on the economy after this week's data, read:

  • Breaking News: UK headline CPI cools, services sector inflation remains sticky. GBP/USD steady
  • Bank of England moves closer to rate cuts. March a real turning point for monetary policy and the Pound

Scenario 1

Cable has been on a downward trend, printing lower highs and lower lows since the peak of 1.38700 printed on January 27, 2026.

The pair has staircased its way lower since then, and has finally breached the medium-term ascending trendline with a daily candle close yesterday.

This sets up a potential drop of as much as 470-odd pips moving forward.

GBP/USD Daily Time Frame, February 19, 2026

Dropping down to a four-hour chart, GBP/USD is printing a fresh low, while the RSI period 14 hovers in oversold territory.

That is a concern and may hint at a pullback in the near term before a bearish continuation.

Keep an eye on the swing highs around 1.3573 and potentially 1.3651, which lines up with the 100-day MA on the H4 chart.

GBP/USD Four-Hour Time Frame, February 19, 2026

Scenario 2

The concern for this setup is the US dollar, which is enjoying a renaissance this week, which has driven a part of this breakout.

Later today, markets will focus on US data releases of initial jobless claims and the December trade surplus report.

If today’s data confirms a narrower-than-expected deficit for December, it would likely boost growth projections for the fourth quarter of 2025 and provide the US dollar with a short-term lift.

Despite this potential for a bounce, the broader outlook for the greenback remains challenged. While the US Dollar Index (DXY) may drift toward the 98.00 level on positive data, a pervasive "sell the rally" sentiment continues to dominate the market.

Many market participants expect the currency’s strength to be fleeting, as long-term expectations for Federal Reserve policy and global trade shifts maintain downward pressure on the dollar's overall trajectory.

This overarching theme around the US dollar is a major concern and could be the one to scupper a potential deeper selloff in GBP/USD.

In order for the bearish setup to be invalidated, a daily candle close above the 1.3700 swing high on the daily chart is needed to put bulls back in control.

GBP/USD Daily Time Frame, February 19, 2026

Safe trades!

Original Post

data.title: Figma: Coast Is Clear To Rebound (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
In just a few short months, many software companies made the hard transition from being AI stock darlings to potential AI roadkill, at immediate risk of being unseated by cheaply vibe-coded applications. As the Q4 earnings season rolls on and we're seeing strong results and outlooks from a spectrum of software stocks, the "SaaSpocalypse" narrative continues to lose more and more credibility.

Figma (FIG), the AI-powered design platform that is taking market share from legacy incumbents like Adobe (ADBE), just soared 15% after reporting healthy Q4 results and issuing a robust outlook for FY26. Of course, those gains still do little to arrest a tremendous correction over the past year, with shares of Figma below its original $33 IPO price and well below the $100 levels at which it changed hands last August. The question for investors now is: can this stock make a full rebound?

I last wrote a neutral article on Figma in December, when the stock was trading in the high $30s. Since then, the stock is down ~30%, even after the recent post-earnings jump. Given Figma's quickly collapsing valuation amid strong growth (which provides ample evidence that AI is a tailwind rather than a headwind), I'm more than enticed to buy Figma on the dip, and I'm raising my rating on the stock to buy.

To me, especially at current decimated share prices, there are a number of bull case tailwinds that support a rebound for Figma. The key catalysts for investors to keep in mind are:

  • Figma is achieving terrific growth at scale. Figma has surpassed a $1 billion annualized revenue run rate, and yet, in spite of this, the company is still showcasing revenue growth accelerating to the high 30%'s/40%'s. This is a clear indication of a company that serves a broad TAM and has greenfield expansion opportunities, especially as it launches more products.
  • Cross-sell and retention momentum is unparalleled, especially as Figma grows its penetration among larger clients. Figma boasts net retention rates over 130%, indicating that the average Figma customer is boosting its spending on the platform by over 30% y/y. That's a powerful source of revenue growth opportunity from within the existing install base alone, not to mention net-new customer additions.
  • Rule of 40- The company balances ~40% revenue growth alongside low/mid-teens pro forma operating margins, giving Figma a "Rule of 40" score above 50, which is rarefied territory for software companies.
  • Past the lockup expiration period- We're now past the company's IPO lockup expiration period in late January, though low share prices now probably mean few insiders are willing to unload their shares. Moving past this date allows investors to focus more on fundamentals.

Originally, my biggest concern with Figma was an overstretched valuation. But now that the stock has deeply corrected since the beginning of the year, this is no longer an issue for Figma. At current post-earnings share prices near $28, Figma trades at a market cap of $13.78 billion. Netting off the $1.69 billion of cash (which includes a small, roughly ~$31 million balance of digital assets) against zero debt gives us an enterprise value of $12.09 billion.

As shown in the chart above, for FY26 Figma has guided to $1.366-$1.374 billion in revenue, reflecting a growth rate of 29-30% y/y. We note the reason that the stock rallied post-earnings is that Wall Street had expected much more modest $1.29 billion in revenue, or 22% y/y growth for the year.

Figma sits at a valuation of 8.8x EV/FY26 revenue. Of course, it's a stretch to call Figma a bargain stock in a market era where double-digit revenue multiples have become rare. But we should also consider the fact of Figma's rapid revenue growth. Even if growth slowed to, say, ~25% in FY27, Figma's valuation multiple would quickly compress to a mid-6s forward multiple of revenue - in line with many established software companies. As recently as last year, growth software stocks with over 30% top-line growth commanded mid-teens valuation multiples, illustrating how much room Figma has to rise if the market normalizes past the current fear of AI.

Stay long here and ride the post-earnings dip higher.

Q4 Download

Let's now go through Figma's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Figma's revenue grew 40% y/y to $303.8 million, smashing Wall Street's expectations of $293.1 million (+35% y/y) by a three-point margin. The wide spread of Figma reflects the growing divide between fear and reality that enterprise software stocks are set for immediate seat losses amid the growing popularity of vibe coding enterprise applications. Also, we note that Figma's revenue growth accelerated two points versus 38% y/y growth in Q3, which is quite a tough feat for a company that has reached Figma's scale.

The core driver of Figma's growth is net customer expansion. Figma continues to attract customers who end up consuming far more than they initially signed up for, which is a great illustration of how Figma's AI credits system is driving expansion. Furthermore, the company notes that customers are adopting multiple products. The company notes that an increasing proportion of Figma's users are adopting AI workflows, adding Figma Design seats on top of their existing Figma Make subscriptions. As shown in the chart below, net dollar retention improved sequentially to 136%, five points higher than Q3 at 131%. Gross revenue retention (before adding the benefits of expansion seats) clocked in at a healthy 97%.

Part of the tailwind here is that Figma continues to appeal to more and more enterprise customers, potentially signaling a shift away from legacy tools like Adobe. The company ended Q4 with 1,405 customers who generate more than $100k in ARR, a sequential add of 153 such customers (versus 133 added in Q3).

We also think Figma's growing base of enterprise customers should provide a bulwark of safety against immediate churn. Larger customers, who ingrain tools like Figma into their daily operations, are far less likely to sustain the operating risk of suddenly cutting over and moving to an internally built, potentially sloppily vibe-coded tool. Figma's accelerating revenue growth, plus its boosted traction with larger customers, serves to further highlight that AI is a net tailwind rather than a risk for the company.

Where we do find Figma a little wanting is in its profit expansion. Pro forma operating margins lost 10 points y/y to 14%, as the company bears the burden of public company costs plus expands its headcount to fuel its growth opportunity.

We're also disappointed that the company's FY26 operating income target of just $100-$110 million implies just an 8% operating margin next year, a slide from 12% full-year margins in FY25. We think the company is setting guidance conservatively and will eventually exceed these targets, but we do think it's critical for Figma to maintain its "Rule of 40" orientation in order to justify an upward correction in its valuation multiples.

Key Takeaways

There's a lot to like about Figma as it heads into FY26 with a robust growth outlook and a rapidly expanding base of customers. Investors have a tremendous chance to buy this rarefied growth stock at a single-digit revenue multiple. Though we're hoping for greater indications of Figma scaling profitably as it grows, I think this is a great juncture to buy Figma on the dip.

data.title: A More Durable Robinhood Emerges (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Robinhood’s (HOOD) business is moving away from the trading-focused business it was originally founded on and becoming more of an asset-gathering business, subscription business, and banking business. This makes the business more stable and less dependent on the ups and downs of cryptocurrency or trading markets.

Increasing deposits will continue to grow the business’s interest income and operating leverage, providing a stable earnings profile. If the company can continue to drive the strong deposit growth into 2026, the business mix will continue to improve. Since my last coverage, when I downgraded the stock to Hold, shares have fallen 26%, but with the business now more diversified and resilient, I believe the risk/reward supports an upgrade to Buy.

Targets 20%+ Net Deposit Growth in FY26

As I see it, Robinhood Markets has expanding net deposits and a diversified topline. This base reduces dependence on trading volumes that are volatile in nature. Robinhood Markets has moved beyond simple transaction-based income into a broad ecosystem.

In FY25, total platform assets grew nearly ~70% YoY to $324 billion. Net deposits hit $68 billion, which represents a 35% growth rate. This influx of capital directly supports financials by increasing the base of interest-earning assets. Interest-earning assets increased 39% amid growth in the cash sweep program, margin, and credit card loan books.

The expansion into subscription models offers stability. The subscribers of Robinhood Gold grew 58% YoY to 4.2 million, while subscription revenue grew 56% to $50 million in Q4. This is a hedge against market downturns. I mean, as market volatility decreases and trading volumes decrease, subscription fees remain unchanged.

Robinhood Markets now operates 11 businesses, deriving over $100 million in annualized topline. In my view, this diversification means that a decline in one sector (like cryptocurrency) may not destabilize the entire topline. Crypto revenue dropped 38% YoY to $221 million. Against this drop, total topline grew 27% to $1.3 billion, and it confirms that Robinhood Markets can stabilize growth even when specific asset classes underperform.

Over 80% of revenue now comes from outside of crypto. The shift minimizes the risk linked with digital asset cycles. Product expansion drives wallet share. The launch of retirement accounts, joint accounts, and banking services supports customers in consolidating assets on HOOD’s platform.

Retirement assets under custody increased 102% to $26.5 billion. Banking adoption indicates solid initial results. Over 50% of funded banking customers enroll in direct deposit. This high attachment rate signals that customers use Robinhood Markets as a primary financial hub instead of only for speculative trading.

Moreover, operational leverage improves as assets are scaling. The TTM gross profit margin is at 92.4%. It is higher than the sector median of 60.31%. This margin profile indicates that Robinhood Markets can add to the top line with minimal incremental cost. As net deposits grow, the interest income derived from these assets flows to the bottom line. The cash sweep program jumped 26% to $32.8 billion. Whereas the margin book increased 113% to $16.8 billion.

These balances derive high-margin interest income. International expansion adds another element of growth. Robinhood Markets launched services in the UK and EU and acquired 0.75 million international customers. Robinhood plans to expand into additional jurisdictions, boosting the total addressable market, and the focus on new markets in 2026 and active traders also supports volume.

Further, equity notional trading volumes increased 68% (to $710 billion). Options contracts traded boosted 38% to 659 million. By targeting active traders with products like futures and index options, Robinhood Markets captures higher trading activity per user. Forward financials benefit from this asset accumulation as interest rates fluctuate; a larger asset base is a buffer.

If trading volumes decline, the interest on $32.8 billion in cash sweeps continues to derive topline. In my opinion, if crypto markets freeze, the 4.2 million Gold subscribers will continue paying fees. The move from a transaction-only model to an asset-gathering model creates more predictable earnings.

As I look forward, the 2026 plan targets another year of 20%+ net deposit growth. Hitting this target increases the base of assets available for monetization. Robinhood targets building a global ecosystem through integrating banking, retirement, and trading. With that, Robinhood Markets increases customer retention. Sticky assets that stay on the platform longer may continue to generate lifetime value.

Revenue Must Exceed 18% Expense Growth to Stabilize 30x P/E

For me, Robinhood Markets has a risk where its market valuation is over its near-term financial delivery capabilities. This is boosted by continued volatility in transactional revenue. A forward P/E (non-GAAP) of 29.4x. This is 175% higher than the sector median of 10.6x. This means that the market demands high growth when financials miss these elevated expectations. HOOD's stock price suffers a big correction, and the Q4 points to this risk.

Robinhood Markets Q4 net revenue of $1.28 billion had missed the consensus estimate of $1.34 billion by ~$60 million. Following this, HOOD stock declined ~8%-10%. The miss came from a decline in transaction-based revenue that lagged analyst estimates. Transaction-based revenue remains a central base of the financial model (totaling $776 million).

Moreover, as I see it, cryptocurrency revenue proves this volatility. Crypto revenue fell 38% YoY to $221 million despite Robinhood Markets maintaining a bullish long-term stance on the industry. When Bitcoin prices/general crypto interest wanes, this topline contracts quickly. Robinhood already acknowledged that falling Bitcoin prices impacted Q4 earnings. So, the dependence on market interest for specific asset classes leads to earnings unpredictability. Expense growth presents another issue to financials.

Robinhood issued guidance for 2026 adjusted OpEx and share-based compensation to range between $2.6 billion and $2.725 billion. This range is an 18% increase at the midpoint against 2025. Rising costs require supporting topline growth to maintain margins. If transaction volumes soften and asset prices decline, topline growth may fail to catch this 18% expense hike.

Further, operating leverage works in both ways. A high-gross-margin structure benefits from volume increases but is hurt as volume drops. Fixed costs for engineering, compliance, and infra remain high regardless of trading activity. If the topline stagnates due to a crypto winter/reduced retail participation, margins compress. Thus, GAAP EPS may have missed estimates by failing to match the revenue projections. Although EPS did beat by $0.02 on a GAAP basis due to other factors.

However, the revenue miss drives the issue. CapEx efficiency also lags the sector median. The CapEx to Sales ratio is 0.34%, which is 79.88% lower than the sector median. Although this suggests a capital-light model, the D- grade implies that investment in physical infra/necessary upgrades might not be enough to match industry standards. Alternatively, it may also indicate that growth relies heavily on marketing and personnel (OPEX) instead of capitalized assets, which keeps ongoing costs high.

Finally, I think the Valuation Grade of D overall points out that HOOD stock is expensive across multiple metrics. Forward P/S is 12.54x, which is 330% higher than the sector median. Overall, these multiples indicate a little room for error. For instance, any deceleration in net deposit growth/active trader participation can trigger a re-rating of the stock. Again, if the market money rotates out of high-growth, high-multiple stocks, Robinhood Markets stock may be hit with downside even amid its operational progress.

Takeaway

Robinhood is transforming into an asset-gathering platform, with 35% deposit growth, 80%+ revenue from outside crypto, and 92% gross margins providing stronger operating leverage. The key is sustaining 20%+ deposit growth into 2026. With a 26% pullback, the improved mix should provide more long-term stability, although high valuation leaves little room for execution error.

data.title: Circle Q4 Earnings Preview: Sell This Stock Before February 25
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate my sell recommendation on Circle (CRCL) shares, holder of the USDC stablecoin. This article is a continuation of my initial coverage article published on July 4, 2025. My intention with this article is to provide an earnings preview, as the company will release its results on February 25.

Context

My last article on Circle was published on July 4, 2025, when I covered its IPO. At the time, despite a very constructive scenario for cryptocurrencies, my recommendation was to sell due to doubts about the attractiveness of the investment. Since then, shares have fallen 67%, while the S&P 500 has risen 9.94%.

The fact is that the company will release its fourth quarter results on February 25th, and I decided to revisit the thesis to check how attractive it is after this sharp drop in shares. Before going into detail about the numbers, it is interesting to note that Circle surprised the market in its last two results.

However, the last quarter marked a sharp worsening of the scenario for cryptocurrencies, and this should be taken into consideration by investors. But before talking about my outlook for the results, it's interesting to take a step back and remember the highlights of Q3.

Q3 Highlights

The company reported 66% YoY revenue growth to $740 million, while adjusted EBITDA margin reached 57%. In parallel, the USDC stablecoin grew 108% YoY to $73.7 billion. Despite this, shares fell more than 7% after the earnings release, largely due to the risks I cited in my coverage article.

It is worth remembering that despite the technology used, Circle's business is almost exclusively dependent on interest in the investments of dollars backed by its stablecoins, and this business can be seen as a commodity. After all, other companies may have a similar stablecoin (perhaps not with the same network effect), but have lower margins, and this creates competition. Therefore, it still doesn't seem attractive to me to pay such a high multiple for a business of this type.

But After All, What Do I Expect From Q4?

After a catastrophic scenario for cryptocurrencies at the end of 2025, it seems to me that we will see the first quarter of stagnation in the sequential growth of USDC in circulation. So don't get carried away by the strong annual growth, but look at the quarterly growth. This growth will be important when analysts compare USDC to its competitors.

In the same sense, reserve income should be stable, or perhaps even below the $711 million reported in Q3. After all, the Fed maintained interest rates between 3.5% and 3.75% throughout the fourth quarter, slightly below the previous quarter.

Regarding distribution costs and margins (the company pays to distribute its stablecoins with companies like Coinbase), it is interesting that the CFO was quite cautious in last quarter's Q&A about the RLDC margin for Q4.

It is worth remembering that distribution costs grew more than revenues in Q3. Therefore, this may be the main number to monitor in the result. If the RLDC margin comes in below 35% in Q4, for example, and therefore below the 39.5% reported in Q3, this will be a sign of higher than normal distribution costs.

All other indicators, such as Other Revenue or Net Income, honestly have little relevance to me, therefore, I will not provide my expectations, as the margin of error is too large and the numbers are irrelevant. Other Revenue, for example, despite showing strong growth in recent quarters, still has a very small base compared to Circle's results as a whole.

According to SA, we have the following main estimates:

What are the risks to my skeptical view?

In general, the big risk for my skeptical view is the company showing resilience even in the face of the last challenging quarter for cryptocurrencies. If USDC circulation exceeds $73 billion, it will show the resilience of USDC. Likewise, if the RLDC margin is above 39%, this will show cost discipline, and of course, there are other drivers, such as the company offering optimistic guidance for 2026.

The Bottom Line

My recommendation to sell Circle shares at the IPO was very assertive. Since then, shares have fallen 67%, while the S&P 500 has risen 9.94%. Therefore, the investor who followed the recommendation saved himself from a huge loss on his investments.

The fact is that the company surprised market expectations in the last two results; therefore, I felt compelled to do an earnings preview, given that the company will release its results on February 25th. However, upon reviewing the business again, I saw little evolution of the case since my last article.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to sell Circle shares before the release of fourth quarter results. Honestly, the excessively concentrated business model, the possibility of increased competition, and the extremely high multiple are still not attractive enough, in my opinion, to recommend buying the shares.

data.title: WTI Crude Oil: Surge To $66 As Iran Tensions Revive Geopolitical Premium
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

​WTI crude oil (CL1:COM) climbed 1.74% to $66.06 per barrel, extending the prior session’s more than 4% rally, the strongest single-day advance since late October. The rebound marks a sharp reversal from recent consolidation and underscores how quickly geopolitical risk can override concerns about oversupply.

The latest gains come as tensions between the U.S. and Iran intensify, injecting a renewed risk premium into global energy markets. Traders who had focused on rising inventories and surplus projections are now recalibrating positions amid the prospect of supply disruptions in the Middle East.

Technical breakout shifts short-term momentum

From a chart perspective, crude oil has staged a decisive breakout. Prices have moved above the 50-day EMA at $63.17, the 100-day EMA at $61.53, the 200-day EMA at $62.68 and the longer 200-day MA at $61.25. That cluster, which had acted as resistance for weeks, now sits firmly below the market.

Bollinger Bands show a middle line at $63.44 and an upper band near $66.7, placing crude close to the top of its recent range. The recovery follows a pullback to the $61-62 zone after February’s rally from December lows near $55 to a high around $67.

Momentum indicators have turned higher, confirming the shift in sentiment. Immediate resistance lies near $66.70 and then $67-68, the February peak. A sustained break above those levels could open the path toward $70. On the downside, the $63-64 area now represents first support, with stronger backing at $61-62.

Geopolitical risks overshadow inventory concerns

The fundamental backdrop has also turned more supportive. Reports indicate that U.S. military planning regarding Iran could involve a prolonged campaign if diplomacy fails. Israel has publicly advocated tougher measures, while U.S. officials have warned that military options remain on the table.

Although Iran has described talks as having reached “general agreement” on guiding principles, senior U.S. officials say key red lines remain unresolved. The uncertainty has renewed fears that crude flows through the Strait of Hormuz, a corridor that handles roughly one-fifth of global oil shipments, could be disrupted.

Inventory data offered modest reassurance. The American Petroleum Institute reported a 0.61 million barrel decline in U.S. crude stocks last week, a stark contrast to the previous week’s 13.4 million barrel build. While small, the draw suggests that the recent surge in inventories may be moderating.

As previously discussed, WTI had struggled to hold above $65 earlier this year as oversupply projections weighed on sentiment. The latest move shows how quickly geopolitical catalysts can alter the narrative, at least in the short term.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Main Street Capital's Valuation Problem: How Interest Rates Could Derail Its Dividend Appeal
data.description:
I have covered Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN) before, where I outlined the investment thesis in detail, and in my last article, explained why I considered it overvalued. Since then, the stock reached my fair value, but after the recent increase, and lower NAV appreciation than I expected, I still consider it overvalued.

Background

Main Street Capital Corporation is a US business development company that provides capital, through debt and equity, to small and mid-sized US businesses.

MAIN divides its investments into four categories: Lower Middle Market (LMM), Private Loan, Middle Market, and other; however, the first two categories represent almost all of its business, accounting for 91% of the investments. Specifically, the LMM segment accounts for approximately 54% of the total investment, while Private Loan accounts for about 37%.

The Lower Middle Market targets companies with annual revenue between $10 million and $150 million and EBITDA between $3 million and $20 million and offers debt and equity financing.

The Private Loan segment consists of debt investments in privately held companies typically owned or being acquired by a private equity fund and targets bigger companies with annual revenue between $25 million and $500 million and EBITDA between $7.5-$50 million.

While most of MAIN’s invested capital is directed to recapitalization or refinancing of companies, in general, the company is very well diversified with investments in 185 different companies, and the largest one represents less than 5% of the total portfolio.

The industry diversification is also great, with no sector with a weight higher than 10%.

MAIN’s main exposure is its interest rate sensitivity. While 75% of the LMM investments are at fixed rates, the Private Loan segment is the opposite, with 96% of investments being at floating rates. This could represent a problem because if rates go down, MAIN's supplemental dividend could be impacted.

In general, 65% of MAIN’s debt investments are at floating rates, while 90% of MAIN’s outstanding debt is at fixed rates. This means that the impact of lower rates is much higher than the benefit for the debt payments.

Moreover, as I will comment in the next section in more detail, MAIN’s cheapest debt matures next year, and new issuance will be more expensive.

This is important, because MAIN’s principal attraction is its monthly dividend and its special dividends.

While I believe that the company will continue to increase its monthly dividend, if rates come down as Trump intends, the special dividend could be frozen or even reduced.

Financial Position and Valuation

MAIN has a strong financial position with $30 million in cash, $2.1 billion in debt, and $4.9 billion in investments. While cash may seem low, the company has access to credit facilities in case it needs to do new investments.

More concerning is that nearly a quarter of its debt matures this July. While I don’t expect MAIN to have any problem issuing new debt, the debt maturing is its cheapest, at a 3% fixed rate. In my last article, I suggested that MAIN could issue debt at a 6% rate. In August the company issued $350 million at 5.4%; however, this lower coupon is because the maturity was just three years out, while I was expecting closer to five. I believe that the company shortened the duration to pay less than its credit facility.

I expect MAIN to use the credit facility, with a cost around 6%, to pay the July maturity and issue debt with short maturities to try to reduce its interest expenses. However, refinancing nearly a quarter of its debt at double the interest expense will weigh on the company’s earnings.

The combination of interest rate headwinds and historically elevated valuations makes me cautious about the stock.

Assuming that it trades back to its last few years mean valuation, the stock's fair value should be $54 per share; however, given the interest rate headwinds, I believe the valuation could decline further.

Risks

I believe that there are two main risks. The first is that valuation continues to increase and the stock trades above 2x NAV. While 2x NAV has been the ceiling so far, there is no assurance that the stock won’t trade higher if sentiment remains strong.

The other risk is that interest rates move higher. In that case, MAIN will benefit, as most of its debt is fixed while its investments have more exposure to floating rates. In that case, the company could continue to increase its monthly dividend and the special dividend, attracting more investors.

Conclusion

MAIN valuation remains at elevated levels, while possible headwinds could halt the dividend increase or, in more extreme scenarios, reduce the special dividend.

When the 2026 notes mature, interest expenses will increase considerably, weighing on the company’s earnings.

Taking that into consideration and assuming its average NAV multiple since 2021, my fair value is $54 per share; however, I consider that there could be more downside.

data.title: NRG Energy: Positioned To Monetize The AI Power Boom
data.description:
Why NRG Sits at the Center of the Grid Transformation

Electricity in the U.S. is a hot topic that keeps intensifying month after month. We are talking about record levels after record levels, as after the estimated 4,195 TWh for 2025, 4,268 TWh are projected for 2026 and 4,372 TWh for 2027, a situation that has clearly become structural.

We can find clues as to what is driving this situation; for example, in Texas, ERCOT has been managing a “large load” request queue of around 205 GW versus a peak demand that was considered historic at around 85 GW.

Basically, this means that just the pipeline of interconnection requests is already roughly equivalent to 2x the maximum load the system has needed on a day of very high intensity.

In PJM, I see a similar tone, as the operator’s plan to manage this surge in demand is that either new consumers, i.e., data centers, start bringing their own generation, or they will have to enter into a scheme where they accept curtailment in case of system stress.

To me, these are figures that practically compete with the total installed capacity of entire countries, which fits perfectly with the upward revisions in CapEx that we are seeing, as well as with grid planning that can support the new environment.

And this is where NRG Energy, Inc. (NYSE:NRG) comes in. That is why I decided to write about it, because in this new scenario it is a piece that fits in a fairly natural way. The business model combines retail, generation, and demand management, but with the closing of the LS Power deal, NRG adds 13 GW of gas generation, practically doubling its capacity to around 25 GW. This scale has led to an update of its guidance for 2026 to a range of $5.32B to $5.82B of adjusted EBITDA and $2.8B to $3.3B of FCF before growth.

There are clearly tailwinds, and the company seems positioned to capture part of this new reality. However, I still need to check how much of this demand actually ends up in cash, and if that is the case, whether we are still on time to get into a company that is indirectly benefiting from the new AI revolution.

Valuation, Leverage, and 12-Month Price Target

As we discussed, I am going to use the midpoint of EBITDA, assuming for this next FY26 around $5.58B, and FCF before growth of $3.05B. Just to clarify the term, FCF before growth refers to the portion of cash that is generated without assuming expansion, and while it is not a GAAP metric, it is a metric used by NRG itself and the market’s main reference point, which is why I use it.

So, which items impact these metrics? I start with the fact that, historically, I see in NRG a significant volatility in OCF driven by working capital due to collateral requirements linked to energy hedges, so when wholesale prices move abruptly higher or lower, working capital ends up absorbing these timing mismatches on a temporary basis. Therefore, to assume an FCF that can be defended in a reasonable way for this FY26, we need an environment where volatility does not exist or can be managed, together with a hedging structure that does not put pressure on the company’s balance sheet.

Leverage also matters, as after the integration of LS Power, net debt sits around the $11B range, so with an estimated EBITDA of about $5.58B, we are looking at a net debt/EBITDA ratio close to 2x. In my view, that is not an alarming level, but it is meaningful enough that a relevant financing cost can show up, and it has to be monitored and quantified. In fact, if the average cost of debt is around 6%, we are talking about roughly $660M per year going straight into interest expense.

Finally, I also look at CapEx, because if guidance is framed in terms of FCF before growth, then maintenance CapEx is already deducted, leaving out expansionary CapEx. Based on what can be seen in the financial statements, in recent years investment levels have been above the pre-COVID period, so with the power system increasing demand year after year, I see no reason to assume CapEx will go back to prior lows. Therefore, my first scenario would look like this:

Adjusted EBITDA

$5.58B

FCF before growth

$3.05B

Diluted shares

192M

FCF per share

$15.9

My next step is to determine what multiple can be assigned, taking cash generation into account, but also the role NRG plays in the current setup, as that also needs to be quantified, even if it is more intangible. Comparable businesses have traded in a 10x to 13x FCF range depending on the energy cycle, so that is the first reference I use to check positioning and determine whether the stock could suggest undervaluation or overvaluation at those levels. Applying that range, we get:

P/FCF multiple

12M price

11x

$175

12x

$191

13x

$207

At current market prices, considering the stock is trading around the $173 per share area and looking at the recent price appreciation and the current market setup, I think there is still room to the upside. That said, in the next report we will need to confirm that the company remains stable in terms of margins, working capital control, and the consistency of converting EBITDA into cash. If that consistency is proven, in my view the multiple should trade toward the upper end of the range, with limited downside, as it would be supported by controlled cash generation.

The Real Risks Behind NRG’s Cash Flow

My main scenario assumes FCF before growth of around $3.05B, so the key risk revolves around the impacts it could take, meaning under what conditions NRG is able to maintain stability in OCF. The company uses financial hedges to lock in electricity and gas prices, but when those positions generate collateral requirements, as we discussed in the previous section, they can create OCF impacts of hundreds of millions of dollars.

If, for example, in 2026 we see abrupt price moves, and a mismatch in the $300M to $500M range shows up in working capital, we would see FCF reduced to a range closer to $2.5B, and that would automatically push us toward the upper end of the valuation range, i.e., 13x.

But it can also happen that, with EBITDA at $5.58B, which is the scenario I am working with, and considering net debt, if NRG saw a margin compression of, for example, 5%, we would be talking about a decline of around -$275M in EBITDA.

After the interest expense we quantified at around $660M and maintenance CapEx, it would be reasonable to justify FCF per share levels around $14.5, or in other words, $174 per share if we apply a conservative 12x multiple.

So the scenario I will be watching is whether these risks begin to surface or whether we start to see an adjustment in the price. In that case, it could be reasonable to set multiples of 11x or 10x, as the market could be pricing in higher financial volatility and a more limited ability to sustain spreads.

Positioned to Monetize the AI Power Boom

My takeaway, after putting together all the information available, is to rate NRG a Buy.

To me, U.S. power demand is a reality that shows up in TWh, in interconnection queues, and in the CapEx revisions we are seeing today. In that setup, NRG is a very interesting piece on the board, as it is not a traditional regulated utility, and it operates more like an operator that can convert the volatility that shows up in the market into margin, as long as it keeps executing the way it has so far.

My base case points to FCF before growth of around $3.05B, and if I consider a balance sheet that is running at roughly 2x net debt/EBITDA, I think the current price of $173 at the time of writing suggests the market is not fully pricing the context in which NRG is operating.

So, in my view, there is an opportunity here. However, as always, we will need to stay focused on the next results to confirm the consistency of converting EBITDA into cash and to monitor how working capital moves.

data.title: Columbia Acorn Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Entering 2026, we believe the current backdrop represents an attractive setup for multi-year outperformance for smaller caps.

Fund strategy

An intrinsic value approach to growth investing

  • Employs a consistent and disciplined process to identify small- and mid-cap companies that possess economic value-creation potential not yet appreciated by the market
  • Seeks attractively valued companies that have strong management, positive and innovative cultures and operate within favorable market structures in growing sectors
  • Supported by an experienced analyst team, specialized by sector or industry, that follows an intensive bottom-up approach to stock

Performance data shown represents past performance and is not a guarantee of future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data shown. Please visit columbiathreadneedleus.com for performance data current to the most recent month end. Institutional Class shares are sold at net asset value and have limited eligibility. Columbia Management Investment Distributors, Inc. offers multiple share classes, not all necessarily available through all firms, and the share class ratings may vary. Contact us for details.

Expense ratio

From the fund's most recent prospectus. Net expense ratio reflects a contractual fee waiver/expense reimbursement through 04/30/2027, unless sooner terminated at the sole discretion of the fund's board.

Fund performance

  • Institutional Class shares of Columbia Acorn Fund (ACRNX) returned 1.24% during the fourth quarter.
  • The fund’s benchmark, the Russell 2500 Growth Index, returned 0.33% for the same period.
  • For monthly performance information, please check online at columbiathreadneedleus.com.

Overview and performance drivers

The fourth quarter saw the Russell 2500 Growth Index finish essentially flat. While lower interest rates and declining headline inflation continued to support markets into year end, a significant Santa Claus rally failed to materialize, and value outperformed growth. Gains were strongest within the health care, real estate and communication services sectors, including a sustained risk-on rally in biotechnology stocks. Conversely, real estate, utilities and information technology posted declines, in the last case, likely due to shorter-term mean reversion and profit-taking.

Our philosophy and process, including a disciplined valuation framework, started to show signs of life following a difficult few quarters of trying to keep pace in a market fueled by more speculative stocks. From a sector perspective, strong stock selection in information technology and consumer discretionary contributed positively to relative returns, while positioning in health care detracted from performance. Our strategy typically underweights biotechnology and pharmaceuticals, choosing to overweight the life sciences “picks and shovels” of health care innovation. However, biopharma’s waves of risk-on momentum and binary clinical trial outcomes can sometimes result in shorter-term headwinds.

Overall, stock selection rather than sector allocation was the primary driver of the fund's outperformance during the quarter. Our leading contributors were primarily within health care and information technology, while our detractors were largely idiosyncratic and spread across a few different industries.

Top holdings (% of net assets): as of December 31, 2025

Celestica (CLS)

2.23

Churchill Downs (CHDN)

2.14

Insmed (INSM)

2.09

GCM Grosvenor (GCMG)

2.06

Carlyle Group (CG)

2.04

Wingstop (WING)

2.02

TopBuild (BLD)

2.00

VSE Corp (VSEC)

1.95

FTAI Aviation (FTAI)

1.83

Cavco Industries (CVCO)

1.80

Top holdings exclude short-term holdings and cash, if applicable. Fund holdings are as of the date given, are subject to change at any time, and are not recommendations to buy or sell any security. Current and future bond holdings are subject to risk.

Top five contributors - Effect on return (%) as of December 31, 2025

Exact Sciences (EXAS)

1.33

Coherent (COHR)

0.77

Insmed

0.43

SPDR S&P Biotech ETF (XBI)

0.41

Celestica

0.39

Leading contributors

  • Exact Sciences is a provider of non-invasive cancer screening and therapy selection tests with its leading stool-based test Cologuard. A long-time holding, the stock outperformed meaningfully during the quarter, following very strong and better-than-expected third-quarter results posted in early November, as well as the announced acquisition by Abbott Laboratories just a few weeks later. Abbott is acquiring Exact Sciences for $105 per share, representing an equity value of approximately $21 billion with an expected close in the second quarter of 2026. We exited Exact Sciences in the weeks following the announcement, but view the combined company as well-positioned to be a leader in global cancer screening and precision oncology diagnostics.
  • Coherent shares rose sharply in the quarter, and the stock remains a compelling holding, strategically positioned as a leader in optical components that enable high-speed datacenter interconnects essential for artificial intelligence (AI) workloads. The company has multiple growth catalysts, including datacenter capacity additions driven by AI initiatives, telecom recovery following inventory digestion, new product commercialization in optical switches and industrial market stabilization. Moreover, we believe the company continues to trade at a reasonable multiple relative to its growth trajectory. Coherent has significant margin expansion opportunities through improving product mix, operational leverage and acquisition synergies.
  • Celestica designs and manufactures essential hardware that powers AI datacenters, working directly with the biggest tech companies (Google, Meta, Amazon) to build custom solutions for specific AI needs, including high-speed switches that connect thousands of AI servers, along with specialized servers and high-capacity storage systems. Over the quarter, Celestica continued to land deals, including one with OpenAI, while further strengthening its position with hyperscaler customers. We continue to see attractive risk/reward in the stock, and it remains a large technology position.

Sector weights (%) fund versus benchmark as of December 31, 2025

Top five detractors - Effect on return (%) as of December 31, 2025

Parsons (PSN) -0.45
ELF Beauty (ELF) -0.39
Janus International Group (JBI) -0.37
Dorman Products (DORM) -0.32
Varonis Systems (VRNS) -0.26

Leading detractors

  • Parsons is a standout in the defense and infrastructure space, with a strong focus on cybersecurity, intelligence and critical infrastructure protection. With a 75-year history, Parsons has built an increasingly differentiated high-end talent base in the control, communications, computers, cyber, intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance spaces as well as in the cyber domain, which complement its legacy positions in missile defense and traditional federal services. It is also the leading player in the Middle East market (20% of revenues). Parsons' stock sold off during the quarter following the news that the company did not win a significant FAA modernization contract. While disappointing, management emphasized that this does not impact the company’s existing FAA business, and they expect to receive significant incremental work on current contracts. We view the market reaction as excessive, given Parsons' strong positioning in growing defense areas like cybersecurity and critical infrastructure, where spending continues to increase.
  • e.l.f. Beauty is a cosmetics and skincare company offering strong value under the e.l.f brand, effective skincare under Naturium and, more recently, premium cosmetics under Rhode. The company provided initial annual earnings guidance well below expectations. The majority of the disappointment was due to the fact the company halted shipments to certain retailers in the quarter due to their delays in implementing tariff-driven August 1 price increases. However, sell-through to end consumers remained strong, and the company continued to take share in the category. We view the sales pause as a smart move to protect the brand and retailer relationships and continue to see potential for significant global expansion through increasing shelf space at existing and new retailers, as well as adding new products (especially for Rhode). The stock has rebounded sharply so far in 2026.
  • Janus International Group is a leading global manufacturer and supplier of turn-key self-storage and commercial and industrial building solutions. The company holds a dominant market position in the self-storage industry, with nearly 70% market share and has been expanding its presence internationally. Janus generates strong free cash flow and maintains solid return metrics driven by its competitive moat in the self-storage market. The stock experienced a significant sell-off due to weaker-than-expected results, driven by some project delays in its self-storage segment related to efforts to implement more uniform branding across subsidiaries. Despite these near-term headwinds, Janus remains attractive, with stable backlogs and construction-ready sites that could begin development within a quarter or two once customers decide to move forward. The company appears well-positioned for recovery once the current macroeconomic uncertainties subside.

Outlook

We found 2025 challenging as small- and mid-cap investors. Given our philosophy and process, we struggled with the dominance of themes and memes driving up valuations, partially due to elevated retail investor participation. In addition, the macroeconomic environment has been choppy and confusing, with changing conditions impacting consumer and corporate sentiment and creating rolling recessions in various pockets of the economy. That said, entering 2026 we believe the current backdrop represents an attractive setup for multi-year outperformance for smaller caps. The combination of more realistic valuations, particularly relative to large caps, easing monetary headwinds and re-emerging merger and acquisition activity should all benefit the group.

As part of our risk management process, we are aware of the themes and factors driving the market, but our investment philosophy and process remain consistent. We remain growth investors focused on holding innovative and high-quality, small- and mid-cap companies trading at discounts to their intrinsic value. We refer to these companies as "classification misfits," as the stock price reflects a misunderstanding of the business model or economic value creation. While this can take some time to play out, we believe market volatility can provide this approach with attractive opportunities.

In recent commentaries, we have discussed our substantial holdings in the AI and defense areas. We remain overweight aerospace and defense, seeing several companies poised to grow faster than anticipated over the long term. In terms of AI, we are underweight the information technology sector, given lower visibility and faster changes to the winners and losers. However, we believe we are actually overweight the sector in broader terms, given our exposure to picks and shovels industrials stocks, which are agnostic to more frequent, incremental technology shifts as AI matures.

Following multiple years of underperformance post COVID, health care finally showed some signs of life in 2025 and was the best-performing sector over the second half. The current period (2024–2027) marks the peak for the U.S. population aging into Medicare, and by 2030, the entire Baby Boomer generation will be over 65. Over the next two decades, these individuals will drive a massive shift and increase in health care demand.

Life sciences diagnostics is an area we’re watching closely with investments in a few companies exposed to the broader themes of longevity and precision medicine. Masimo, a global med-tech that develops noninvasive patient-monitoring technologies, and Glaukos, a leading ophthalmic med-tech focused on chronic eye diseases, are two large holdings that benefit from aging demographics. Both have idiosyncratic drivers of growth and exciting product cycles over the next few years. Overall, we believe the recent improvement in biopharma funding and sentiment will continue to work its way through the broader health care ecosystem in 2026, providing an attractive multi-year growth outlook for a sector that is relatively insulated from broader macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty.

In sum, the fund is positioned with a below-benchmark risk profile, which has hurt in the recent environment, as higher volatility stocks have outperformed. However, we believe our positioning is prudent and positions the fund to outperform as the “animal spirits” revert to their historical mean. We are sticking to our process of identifying acorns that we can hold until they are mighty oak trees. We believe the fund is well-positioned as we seek to achieve our goal of outperforming on a rolling three-year basis, while taking less risk than the benchmark.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a mutual fund carefully before investing. For a free prospectus or a summary prospectus, which contains this and other important information

Investment Risks

Market risk may affect a single issuer, sector of the economy, industry or the market as a whole. Investments in small- and mid-cap companies involve risks and volatility greater than investments in larger, more established companies. Foreign investments subject the fund to risks, including political, economic, market, social and others within a particular country, as well as to currency instabilities and less stringent financial and accounting standards generally applicable to U.S. issuers. Risks are enhanced for emerging market issuers. The fund may invest significantly in issuers within a particular sector, which may be negatively affected by market, economic or other conditions, making the fund more vulnerable to unfavorable developments in the sector.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a mutual fund carefully before investing. For a free prospectus or a summary prospectus, which contains this and other important information about the funds, visit Financial Professionals | Columbia Threadneedle Investments US. Read the prospectus carefully before investing.

Actual investments or investment decisions made by CMIA and its affiliates, whether for its own account or on behalf of clients, may not necessarily reflect the views expressed. This information is not intended to provide investment advice and does not take into consideration individual investor circumstances. Investment decisions should always be made based on an investor's specific financial needs, objectives, goals, time horizon and risk tolerance. Asset classes de- scribed may not be appropriate for all investors. Past performance does not guarantee future results, and no forecast should be considered a guarantee either. Since economic and market conditions change frequently, there can be no assurance that the trends described here will continue or that any forecasts are accurate.

Columbia Acorn funds are distributed by Columbia Management Investment Distributors, Inc., member FINRA and managed by Columbia Management Investment Advisers, LLC.

data.title: Globalstar: This Price Level Has No Margin Of Safety
data.description:
Thesis review

Right after the publication of my article on Globalstar (GSAT), SpaceX (SPACE) announced the acquisition of EchoStar (SATS) spectrum assets for $17 billion. This deal raised eyebrows for most investors, and we started seeing rumors spreading that the next acquisition target could be GSAT. This rumor resulted in GSAT rallying by more than 19% in a single day. To date, the stock price has risen by almost 100% since my sell rating. Clearly this article didn't age well and got me thinking hard about what I could have missed in my initial analysis.

My main concern about GSAT was that it sources the majority of its revenue from Apple, and given that we are seeing an increasing number of SAT companies aggressively pursuing the D2D market, this could result in Apple switching to a better provider, likely SpaceX, which could cut GSAT revenue by more than 60%. This level of risk doesn't justify the premium valuation GSAT is currently trading at. I have noted down a few rebuttals to my bearish take, and they have compelled me to rerate GSAT from a sell to a hold.

The first rebuttal to my bearish take is that even though GSAT has a customer concentration risk, the partnership with Apple is very pivotal to both entities, and given the scale of Apple's operation and the amount of free cash it generates in a given year, the reimbursement obligations are a tiny drop, and Apple would rather expand the base of its satellite service providers than switch from one to another. The only thing that could put the contract agreement at risk of termination is if GSAT fails to provide a better service to Apple after it has launched replacement satellites and fully upgraded and expanded its MSS infrastructure. These are medium- to long-term milestones, and in the meantime, GSAT will continue to enjoy cash injections from Apple.

The second rebuttal is that, suppose the contract gets terminated, this will definitely impact GSAT's day-to-day business operations, but the spectrum assets GSAT has will remain attractive to other partners and competitors. Either GSAT will be able to secure another agreement of equal value from other potential partners (mobile network operators like AT&T), or SpaceX could acquire its spectrum assets. I'm highly skeptical of SpaceX acquiring GSAT right now that the Apple agreement is still in place; firstly, it puts Apple in a vulnerable position of relying on one major supplier for its satellite services. I don't think Apple's management would be happy with that, so my estimate is that reality continues to be the same between GSAT and Apple in the medium term. So for further analysis, it's more prudent to look at GSAT's operational fundamentals like revenue growth, margins, and free cash flow than try to estimate the value of its spectrum assets.

Financial Analysis

When we look at the period between 2016 and 2022, we can see that GSAT was operating at thin cash flow margins, had massive debt on its balance sheet that it was actively reducing, and was running on low cash and low capital expenditure needs. Operating cash flow started ticking up in 2022, and the firm began increasing its debt balance again. After the announcement of the partnership, we saw operating cash flow, CAPEX, and cash reserves hitting all-time highs, illustrating the capital accretion value of this deal. Since September of 2022, when the agreement was announced, operating cash flow and CAPEX have increased by more than 1000%, from $56.42 million and $39.21 million to $786.42 million and $629.70 million, respectively. In the same period, long-term debt increased 85%, from $262.18 million to $485.08 million, and cash increased by more than 2000%, from $14.75 million to $342.29 million. This amount of cash influx is a pivotal change for this firm, as it allows it to further reinvest in more satellite launches and ground infrastructure build-out. Because the cash from the Apple deal comes in the form of cost reimbursement for capital expenditure, it makes sense that the company would raise more debt to finance its CAPEX and then later use the reimbursement funds to pay interest expenses. YoY GSAT interest expenses have increased 114% to $29.66 million on a trailing basis, which is in line with the debt increase seen on the company's balance sheet.

Now it's worth mentioning that these capital inflows come as deferred revenue that will later be recognized as actual revenue over the extended period of time. During the Covid times, GSAT witnessed revenue declines with a YoY growth rate of -8% in Q1 of 2021, but this trend immediately inflected in 2022, and we saw growth rates breaking the 20% and 40% levels between 2022 and 2024. The growth rate has since moderated and to date sits at 8.54%. From Q3 of 2022 to Q3 of 2025, GSAT revenue rose 85%, from $141.67 million to $262.2 million, illustrating value accretion since the deal happened. Gross and EBIT margins also expanded from 54.90% and -42.92% to 65.43% and 4.09% in the same period, respectively. FCF margins have been fluctuating but recently turned positive; this is one metric I am slightly concerned about given that GSAT is not an asset-light business; therefore, it's difficult to estimate how much CAPEX would need to be invested and for how long until the firm can finally return capital to shareholders on a consistent basis.

When we compare GSAT's margins to its sector and industry peers, we can see that the FCF margin massively underperforms peers (-173% vs. the sector median of 10%), and when we look at EBIT margins, there's a bifurcation of peers that earn margins ranging between -4% and 10% and peers that earn margins ranging between 14% and 30%, resulting in a median margin of 11.69%. GSAT falls among the peers that earn a lower EBIT margin (4.09%). Overall, GSAT got rated an F for the profitability factor.

EBITDA margins do paint a better profitability picture for GSAT relative to peers, with EBITDA margins of 38.23% vs. the sector margin of 19.67%, and the firm has outperformed most of its peers in topline growth in the past 5 years. Overall, Iridium (IRDM) surpasses GSAT in most profitability metrics and has the highest EBITDA margin of all its peers, but because topline growth is lagging, the stock price has underperformed GSAT, and this is reflected by a B+ momentum rating for IRDM vs. an A+ rating for GSAT.

GSAT's topline growth has mostly come from one segment of its business, wholesale services, with roughly 60% of overall revenue coming from the Apple deal. I expect this partnership to continue being the main driver of topline growth, as it gives GSAT pricing power without impacting Apple's bottom line. Since Q3 of 2022, wholesale revenue has grown 489%, from $27.59 million to $162.59 million. To give a perspective of this massive growth, the government segment grew by 30%, commercial IoT grew by 42.62%, and SPOT revenue declined by 18.18% in the same period.

This lands the point home that so much of GSAT's value lies in its partnership with Apple and the 85% constellation of its LEO assets that are making this partnership possible. The company is using some of these funds to increase the value and scale of its terrestrial infrastructure to accelerate the commercialization of XCOM RAN. XCOM RAN is a 5G private network that promises to deliver 4x the network capacity of the standard 5G network, which will be a game changer for companies in high demand for low latency and high bandwidth connections for their automation and robotic needs. It's difficult to estimate the impact of XCOM RAN on GSAT fundamentals, as this segment is pre-revenue; however, management estimates they will likely see 50% EBITDA margins once sales start coming in.

Q3 2025 Earnings results

Q3 Income Statement

Looking at the Q3 earnings results, revenue increased 2% YoY to $73.85 million, beating estimates by $4.91 million. Cost of revenue increased 18% to $25.63 million, resulting in gross profit declining 5% to $48.22 million and landing margins at 65.29% vs. 53.96% from the sector median. Operating expenses decreased by 8%, with the main driver being SBC decreasing by 41%, followed by D&A decreasing by 3%. This led to operating income increasing 8% to $10.16 million. Interest expenses increased 284% to $11.03 million amid massive currency losses, resulting in finance expenses increasing 467% and pretax income decreasing 81% to $2.19 million. Tax expenses decreased by 35%, leading to a net income of $1.09 million and net margins of 1.48%.

9-Month Cash Flow Statement

Looking at the 9-month cash flow statement, operating cash flow increased by 352% YoY to $445.77 million, with $340.16 million coming from deferred revenue. Despite this massive cash influx, the company still realized negative free cash flow of $36.13 million due to CAPEX needs that increased by 395% to $481.89 million. Most of this capital expenditure is in preparation for the launch of replacement satellites that are estimated to hit orbit in the first half of 2026.

Q3 2025 Balance Sheet

The balance sheet remains healthy, with cash increasing 567% to $346.3 million and debt increasing 35% to $485.1 million, and deferred revenue of $672.8 million, which increased non-current liabilities by 303% to $1.6 billion.

Valuation

Investors love a company that is stacking more cash in its balance sheet, especially when it operates in a capital-intensive industry like this one; this reduces bankruptcy and insolvency risks and gives it leverage to fund growth. Which explains the premium valuation. GSAT's sales multiple trades at a massive premium relative to peers, 29 vs. the sector median of 1.9, while growing YoY revenue at a similar rate. As a result, the quant system has graded GSAT an F for the valuation factor, which I am in favor of.

But when we look at the cash flow multiple, the stock trades fairly with peers at 9.69 vs. the sector multiple of 7.36. This means investors are valuing GSAT for its ability to generate cash. I personally don't think this is the right metric to gauge with, as most of this cash is from deferred revenue and doesn't speak to the company's ability to increase the value of its product and services.

Risks

GSAT is about to release its Q4 results next week. The main risk I see here that could potentially lead to a price drop is if the company delays the launch of replacement satellites. This is likely to happen as supply chain issues continue to be persistent at the margins, and given that the firm outsources the manufacturing and the launching of satellites, most of these factors are outside its control.

Conclusion

Addressing the main concern I had for GSAT about customer concentration risk, I have come to the conclusion that this risk is not as severe as I initially thought, rebutting the bearish take I had on this name. I still think GSAT is overpriced relative to peers, and at these price levels, I don't see any further upside unless we start seeing revenue from the commercialization of XCOM RAN. This is still in the speculative phase, and management stated it doesn't expect to realize XCOM RAN revenue in the fiscal year of 2025. Hence, I urge investors to hold and wait for earnings results for better clearance going into 2026.
